full_text:
16.12.2021 Anastasiya Chernenko

TISSUE FIXATION AND PROCESSING, FFPE BLOCKS STORAGE

Here is a summary of what I would suggest for improving the blocks' and possibly FFPE DNA/RNA quality. Since FFPE DNA quality was good from Terva samples and, according to Anni, morphology of the sections is very good - we should learn from Kauppi lab's protocol.

According to Anni, sections from Terva blocks look very good, size of the tissue is slightly smaller, fat well trimmed off. We could pay more attention at trimming the fat off. I remember for how long Taina was 'cleaning' the selected tissue piece in the laminar!

Thickness of the tissue to be fixed could be a bit smaller, Anni suggested approx 3mm. I read from some protocol that up to 4mm is recommended, not thicker. This we can pay attention at as well.

Kauppi lab fixes tissues in the histology cassettes, with +4oC cold 4% formaldehyde-PBS.

In the morning while preparing for sample collection they dilute Formaldehyde solution, min 37%, Merck, Cat 1.03999.2500 (stock stored at RT) 1:10 in PBS and then put it to the fridge.

Dilution of the fixative is done as follows:

15ml formaldehyde stock + 135ml PBS, so 1:10. It means that the final formaldehyde concentration is approx 3.7-4%.

We do this dilution the same way. (Though, in Kauppi protocol it says 10% formalin solution. It is because 10% formalin equals to approx 4% formaldehyde. For example, in HUS they use formalin which is ready to be used, from bigger canisters. If i remember right, it is called 10% formalin, most probably neutral buffered.

Could you, Elina, take the picture of this canister in näytehuone hood if you happen to go there before me?)

UPD: in NKL Näytehuone: 1G2256, 10% FORMALIINI puskuroitu, sis. 4% formaldehydiä, fofsaattipuskuroitu, pH 7.2. Reagena.

So, I think, fixing reagents are essentially the same in our lab, in Kauppi lab and in HUS.

The difference is in the temperature of fixation (we do it at RT, cross-linking happens faster) and the duration of the fixation. Slightly smaller tissue pieces and better fat trimming.

When going to pick the sample - jar with fixative is put on ice. So, the fixative is kept cool.

Fixation is done in the cold room, no rocker, for 24-72h, not that strict, but usually over 24h, basically, depending on the weekend approaching.

Then the cassette with fixed tissue is transferred to 70% EtOH and is kept at 4°C until ready for processing in the Tissue Services, Anatomy (standard processing).

So, a suggested protocol:

1. Prepare fresh fixative dilution in the morning, 1:10 in PBS, put to the fridge to cool, then into the ice box when going to pick the sample.

2. First, snap-freeze the DNA/RNA/Protein pieces (time matters) then - trim the selected piece thoroughly from the fat, thickness of the slices keep around 3mm.

3.Put the ready tissue piece into the histology cassette and into the jar with a chilled fixative.

4.Fix in the cold room for 48-72h, standing on the shelf.

5. Transfer the tissue cassette into cold 70% ethanol, put into the fridge.

6. After 1-2 weeks of accumulating cassettes in the ethanol - bring them for standard tissue processing at Tissue Services, Anatomy.

7. When blocks are sectioned - first trim them well. Anni commented that our blocks are 'undertrimmed'.

NOTE:

Formalin is the name for saturated (37%) formaldehyde solution. Thus, a protocol calling for 10% formalin is roughly equivalent to 4% formaldehyde. Beware though, that some solutions have methanol in them to stop polymerization but this could have a negative effect on your sample.

Both our's and Kauppi lab reagents contain Methanol: 12% in our stock and 10% in Kauppi stock. I do not think it really matters, as basically methanol is also a fixing agent, it modifies primary amines the same way as formaldehyde. But if we want to be 100% safe, we could order the same formaldehyde as Kauppi lab.

Another NOTE from Anni:

When we have enough of the sample, we should make two cassettes from the same patient:

one with a smaller and thinner, well trimmed piece (like they take at Pathology for MoPa -molecular pathology analyses, incl. genetic analyses, maximally matching the FF-piece)

second piece - bigger, larger area, but not thicker then 3mm, for stainings.

Let's try this updated procedure starting from the next sample. And mark in all the sample info places that this was the tissue treated with a changed protocol. If blocks look better, we can stick to it and correct our Samples Processing SOP.

I could also then compare FFPE DNA quality from older and newer blocks, if we decide to do so

21.1.2022

Here are some good guidelines for tissue fixation: https://www.medicine.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/Fixation-and-Fixatives.pdf

Also attached. Scroll down.

Nucleic acids preservation is not discussed here, but tissue morphology - yes. Temperature (its increase) will increase speed of fixation, as fixative diffuses faster and chemical reactions happen faster. Low temp not discussed here.

And yes, tissue piece max 3-4 mm thick. Also volume of fixative must be sufficient, 20:1 ratio at least, better 50:1. I would say, volume of our tissue pieces is under 1ml (1 cm3), but if we make two blocks, we should either move them to 100ml of fixative or fix in two separate 50 ml volumes.

According to the tissue fixation guidelines file above, 24h fixation should be enough for the size of our tissue pieces.

(Penetration rate: The penetration rate of a fixing agent depends on its diffusion characteristics and varies

from agent to agent. As devised by Medawar it can be expressed as d = K√t, where d is the depth of

penetration, K is the coefficient of diffusion (specific for each fixative), and t is the time. 1 In practical terms this means that the coefficient of diffusion (K) is the distance in millimeters that the fixative has diffused into the tissue in one hour. For 10% formalin K = 0.78. This means that your formalin fixative should not be expected to penetrate more than say 1 mm in an hour and it will take approximately 25 hours to penetrate to the centre of a 10 mm thick specimen , i.e. 5 mm ( = 5² hours).)

Cold fixation:

https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fonc.2020.00173/full

Cold Formalin Fixation Guarantees DNA Integrity in Formalin Fixed Paraffin Embedded Tissues: Premises for a Better Quality of Diagnostic and Experimental Pathology With a Specific Impact on Breast Cancer

They isolated DNAs from tissues derived from the same surgical resection but processed with two different fixation protocols (standard fixation vs. cold fixation at 4°C) and compared DNA quality.

For Cold Fix the sample was immersed in pre-cooled 4% NBF (neutral buffered formalin) and fixed at 4°C for 24 h.

Cold formalin fixation may be able to reduce both the first enzymatic degradation of DNA by blocking the DNAse activity (20) and the formalin-dependent crosslinks that lead to DNA fragmentation (21).

In Breast Cancer: The fat tissue the mammary gland is composed of may hamper an efficient formalin fixation, yet our results seem to suggest that breast cancer specimens may benefit from cold formalin fixation.

ALSO lower temperature slows down RNases which is crucial for RNA preservation.

To conclude:

24h fixation at +4oC should be enough

Better, if there is a gentle swirling

Volume of fixative can be increased: (Kauppi lab: 150ml volume, 135ml PBS+15ml stock Formaldehyde solution, min 37%, Merck, Cat 1.03999.2500 )

OTHER OPTIONS TO CONSIDER:

Fix with PAXgene? (formalin-free, non-crosslinking fixative)

The PAXgene Tissue System uses an acidic alcoholic fixation without formalin that does not result in cross-linking of biomolecules.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3218013/

PAXgene protocol and comparison with FFPE and FF: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27402607/

Store paraffin blocks at +4oC , better DNA and RNA quality!

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6128582/

Moved paraffin blocks to cold room 1.2.2022NC
